short.headline: India is getting hotter, its rain is swinging, and the air is often unsafe, but the country’s role in causing it is still small.
short.dek: Data on temperature, rainfall, sea level, extreme heat, and emissions paint a picture of a country already feeling the effects, while its per-person CO₂ remains far below that of rich nations.
short.body: Since the 1940s, India’s average temperature has risen about 1.5°C. The sea has climbed 23 cm at Mumbai and 32 cm at Chennai. Warm, humid nights are more common in coastal cities. Meanwhile, India emits 2.2 tonnes of CO₂ per person, less than a sixth of the American figure. But with 145 crore people and 42% of workers still on farms, the exposure is enormous. This page walks through 28 charts that together show the signal, the cause, and the stakes.

macha.heading: Ab kya, macha? What does all this data actually mean for us?
macha.body: Every chart here answers one part of the hot, urgent question: what is changing, and who will feel it. The warming is real, not just in the red stripes, but in the way the monsoon is getting jumpy and the Arabian Sea is inching up the shore. The air that makes you cough in Mumbai traffic is laced with the same smoke that is cooking the planet. But when you look at the fairness numbers, you see that an average Indian is still a tiny emitter compared to someone in America or Europe. That does not make the problem smaller; it makes the conversation trickier. The real punch comes from the two charts at the end: 42% of India still works in farming, and we are now 145 crore people. That is why even a little more heat, a little more rain unpredictability, hits hard.
macha.soWhat: India is living the changes, but it did not fill the atmosphere, and with 145 crore people, every extra degree matters.

article.title: How is climate change changing India?
article.standfirst: A readable walk through the most important data, from the temperature rise you can already feel to the CO₂ that is still rising, and why fairness matters.
article.bodyMarkdown:
India’s climate is shifting in measurable ways. The national average temperature has climbed about 1.5°C since the 1940s. The rainfall that feeds farms and fills reservoirs is swinging more wildly. The Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal are creeping up the land. And the same fossil-fuel smoke that warms the planet chokes the air people breathe. But India’s part in the cause is small per person and historically tiny next to the countries that industrialised first. Here is what the numbers say, chart by chart.

## How much hotter has India actually become?

The chart does not simply show the temperature. It shows the temperature anomaly, the gap between each year’s average and the 1991–2020 baseline. That is a careful way of saying: a value of 0.07°C in 2025 sounds tiny, but it is measured against a period that was already warmer than the mid-20th century. In 1940, the anomaly was –0.92°C. By 2024, it reached +0.57°C. That is a swing of roughly 1.5°C. And because this is a national average, it smooths out the spikes that a labourer in Nagpur or a family in a Delhi slum actually endures. The average is gentler than the reality. The real danger is in the hottest days, which are growing in number.

## What does a century of warming look like?

Climate stripes transform the same anomaly numbers into a field of colour: blue for cooler-than-normal years, red for hotter. In the first half of the record, blue dominates. Then the stripes turn a persistent, deepening red. There are no axes, no labels, just the colour. The message is instant: the last few decades are unlike anything India’s instruments recorded before. The warming is not a theory. It is a colour that keeps getting stronger.

## Is each decade really hotter than the last?

Averaging the anomalies into decades removes the year-to-year noise and reveals the staircase. The 1940s and 1950s were around 0.7°C below the 1991–2020 norm. The 1960s were still below, but less so. After the 1970s, the decades crossed into positive territory and kept climbing. The 2010s were around 0.3°C above the baseline. The single warmest years, 2016, 2017, 2018, all sit inside the two most recent decades. The data does not reverse; each decade has been warmer than the one before it.

## What is India’s actual average temperature, not just the change?

Anomalies measure the change, but they do not tell you how hot it really is. The ERA5 dataset gives the land-only average temperature in degrees Celsius. In 1940, the average was 22.96°C. In 2024, it was 24.46°C. That is a rise of around 1.5°C, consistent with the anomaly shift, but expressed in absolute terms. Twenty-four degrees might sound pleasant, but this is an average across the whole country, from the Himalayas to the Thar Desert, and across all seasons. The figure hides the killing heat of May and June, just as it hides the frozen peaks. It is a single number that helps measure the overall system, not the local experience.

## Where is India’s temperature headed by 2100?

Climate models project a range of futures depending on how much the world emits. The middle-of-the-road scenario (SSP2-4.5) takes India’s average from 24.67°C in 2015 to 26.64°C by 2100, a further rise of about 2°C. Under high emissions, it reaches 29.25°C. Even in the low-emissions vision, it climbs to 25.66°C. These are national averages. The extra 2°C to 5°C on the average means far more scorching days and nights in the cities where most Indians will live. The path is not fixed; it depends on the world’s collective choices, but the direction is clear.

## Why does humidity make the heat more dangerous?

A hot day is worse when the air is thick with moisture, because the body cannot cool itself through sweat. India’s average relative humidity, computed from ERA5 reanalysis, has risen from 51.5% in 1940 to 64.2% in 2024. That is a significant shift for a national mean over a tropical landmass. It means the same 24°C average now feels heavier and more uncomfortable. In coastal cities, where humidity is naturally high, a seemingly moderate temperature can become lethal during a heatwave. Heat and humidity together is the real threat.

## Is India’s rainfall becoming more erratic?

The annual precipitation total does not show a simple up or down trend. It swings. In 1940, the average was 1,211.8 mm. By 2025, it reached 1,404.4 mm. But look at the years in between: 1,110 mm in 2018, then 1,374.7 mm in 2019, then 1,315.4 mm in 2020. The monsoon is the beating heart of India’s farms and water supply, and the rainfall is growing noisier. A warmer atmosphere holds more moisture, so when it rains, it often pours harder, even if the annual total looks similar. What matters for a kharif crop is not the year-end sum but the timing and intensity of the rainfall within the season. This chart shows the annual total to reveal that even the big-picture number is unsteady.

## What is the air quality like in India’s big cities right now?

A live snapshot from the World Air Quality Index project: Mumbai’s AQI is 156 (unhealthy), Delhi’s is 95 (moderate), Chennai 73, Kolkata 65, Bengaluru 58. These numbers change hour by hour, but they capture a typical picture. The dominant pollutant is PM2.5, fine particles that enter the lungs and bloodstream. The same combustion that pumps out CO₂ also releases these particles. So the climate story and the air-quality story share a common root.

## How has air pollution changed over the years?

The World Bank’s estimate of mean annual PM2.5 exposure for India was 62 µg/m³ in 1990. It fell to 48.4 µg/m³ by 2020. That is a drop, but 48 µg/m³ is still more than nine times the WHO guideline of 5 µg/m³. So the air has become marginally cleaner on average, likely due to cooking fuel transitions and some emission controls, but it remains dangerously polluted for millions. The long-term trend adds critical context to the daily AQI snapshot.

## Which cities are seeing more extremely hot days?

The national average temperature rise does not translate into more extremely hot days in every city. Delhi’s count of days at or above 35°C fell from 144 in 1940 to 87 in 2025. Chennai’s dropped from 28 to 21. Mumbai, Kolkata, and Bengaluru rarely cross 35°C. But this single threshold does not capture the full danger, as the next chart explains.

## Are nights staying hot as well?

Yes, warm nights are climbing. In Chennai, the number of warm nights has shot up from 22 in 1940 to 99 in 2025. Mumbai rose from 2 to 14. Delhi saw a decline from 49 to 37, but the data for northwestern cities can be noisy. Hot nights are a hidden killer: without a cool night, the body cannot recover, and the risk of heatstroke multiplies. The rise in humid nights, especially in coastal cities, is one of the clearest climate signals in India’s urban data.

## How much is the sea rising along India’s coasts?

Tide gauges in Mumbai since 1878 and Chennai since 1916 show a steady upward march. At Mumbai, sea level has gone from –40 mm relative to the 1961–1990 average to +192 mm in 2024, a rise of 232 mm over 146 years. At Chennai, the rise is even steeper: from 2.5 mm in 1916 to 317.5 mm in 2022. These are not projections; they are instrument readings. A rising sea means more frequent coastal flooding, saltwater intrusion into groundwater, and higher storm surges during cyclones.

## How many lives do climate-linked disasters claim?

The disaster deaths dataset, compiled by the Centre for Research on the Epidemiology of Disasters, shows floods as the dominant killer in recent years. In 2025, floods took 405 lives, landslides 154, and storms 158, totalling 717. In 2024, extreme temperature deaths spiked to 733 alongside 285 flood and 388 landslide deaths. The numbers bounce around; a single large cyclone or heatwave can cause a spike. The long-term story is that floods and storms are the recurring threat, and as rainfall becomes heavier and seas rise, the odds of a deadly event tilt upward.

## How much CO2 does India now emit?

India released 3.19 billion tonnes of CO₂ from fossil fuels and industry in 2024. That is a thousand times higher than the 3,94,481 tonnes emitted in 1858, the first year in the record. The climb has been steep since the 1990s as the economy grew. But total CO₂ tells only one side, because India also emits other warming gases.

## Is CO2 the whole story, or are other gases also rising?

Greenhouse gases include methane (CH₄) and nitrous oxide (N₂O), not just CO₂. In 2024, India’s emissions included 3.09 billion tonnes of CO₂, 859 million tonnes of CO₂-equivalent from methane, and 310 million from nitrous oxide. Methane comes largely from agriculture and waste. So the total greenhouse gas footprint was 4.26 billion tonnes of CO₂-equivalent. CO₂ is the largest piece, but methane is a quarter of the warming effect. Ignoring it misses a chunk of the problem.

## Which sectors produce India’s greenhouse gases?

In 2023, the largest emitting sector was electricity and heat, at 1.55 billion tonnes of CO₂-equivalent. Agriculture followed with 808 million tonnes, then manufacturing and construction at 675 million, transport at 362 million, buildings at 240 million, and industry at 221 million. The pattern is different from rich countries: agriculture is the second-largest source, reflecting the large rural population and livestock. Land-use change and forestry actually subtracted 146 million tonnes, acting as a sink. This mix shapes which solutions can work.

## Is India’s economic growth becoming less polluting?

Yes, the economy is growing slightly faster than emissions. In 1990, India produced $1.91 trillion in GDP (constant 2015 US$) and emitted 578 million tonnes of CO₂. By 2024, GDP reached $14.25 trillion and CO₂ emissions were 3.19 billion. That means the economy grew about 7.5 times while emissions grew 5.5 times. The carbon intensity of the economy, emissions per dollar of output, has fallen. So there is a relative decoupling, but not an absolute one yet. Emissions are still rising, just not as fast as GDP.

## Is each unit of electricity getting cleaner?

The carbon intensity of India’s electricity, grams of CO₂ emitted per kilowatt-hour generated, has fallen from 740 in 2000 to 670 in 2025. That is a 9% drop over 25 years, driven by more renewables and improved coal plant efficiency. However, 670 g/kWh is still a fairly high number globally. The direction is right, but the pace needs to quicken if electricity is to stop driving up total emissions.

## How is India generating its electricity?

In 2025, coal provided 70.8% of total generation (1,474 TWh out of 2,082 TWh). Clean sources, solar, wind, hydro, nuclear, bioenergy, together supplied 26.7%. Solar alone generated 97 TWh, wind 74 TWh, hydro 178 TWh. Coal still rules, but the clean share has expanded from single digits two decades ago. The transition is visible in the lines: solar is the fastest-growing source, but demand is growing so quickly that even large clean additions have not shrunk coal’s share much.

## How fast are solar and wind capacity growing?

Solar installed capacity jumped from a negligible 1.1 megawatts in 2000 to 97,384 megawatts (97.4 GW) in 2024. Wind rose from 941 MW to 48,163 MW over the same period. Total renewable capacity reached 204 GW. This is one of the world’s fastest energy build-outs, and it has happened mostly in the last ten years. Capacity is not generation, the sun does not always shine, but it shows the foundation being laid.

## Does coal still dominate?

Yes. As a share of generation, coal stood at 70.8% in 2025. The clean share, including hydro and nuclear, reached 26.7%. The headline “clean energy surge” is true in absolute terms, but the grid still runs on coal. The tension between rising demand and the need to displace fossil fuels is the central energy challenge.

## How do India’s per-person emissions compare?

In 2024, an average Indian emitted 2.2 tonnes of CO₂. A Chinese person emitted 8.7 tonnes. An American emitted 14.2 tonnes. The world average was 4.7 tonnes, and the European Union was at 5.4 tonnes. India’s per capita figure is among the lowest of large economies. This is the fairness number: climate change is a global problem, but Indians have contributed far less per person to the cause.

## How do India’s total emissions stack up globally?

India’s 3.19 billion tonnes of CO₂ in 2024 placed it third behind China (12.29 billion) and the United States (4.9 billion), and ahead of the EU (2.43 billion). So India is a significant emitter in absolute terms, but it is home to about 18% of the world’s population, so its share of global emissions (8.3%) is still lower than its population share.

## Who is responsible for the CO2 already in the air?

Cumulative emissions since 1750 are the real measure of historical responsibility. The United States has emitted 435 billion tonnes, China 285 billion, the EU 301 billion. India’s cumulative total is 66 billion tonnes. The atmosphere cares about the stock, not the flow. The big numbers from the early industrialisers are the ones still trapping heat.

## How have per-person emissions grown over time?

A comparison of trajectories since 1750 shows that India’s per capita emissions were near zero until the mid-20th century and have only recently crossed 2 tonnes. The US and UK, by contrast, were emitting over 10 tonnes per person by the early 1900s. China’s rose in the 2000s. The chart makes clear that India’s emissions growth is recent, and its per person figure is still below the world average.

## How much of the world’s CO2 does India emit?

India’s share of global CO₂ emissions was 0.1% in 1858. It reached 8.3% in 2024. That is a steep climb in the last two decades, driven by coal-powered growth. But it is still well below India’s share of the global population. The world’s carbon budget is limited, so this share will be watched closely.

## Why is India so vulnerable: how many still farm?

In 1991, 63.1% of India’s workforce was in agriculture; by 2025, that share had fallen to 41.6%. That is still over 40% of workers depending on the sky for their income. A failed monsoon, an unseasonal downpour, or a blistering spring heatwave can wipe out a year’s livelihood. The shift away from farming is slow, and while it continues, a huge number of people remain directly exposed to climate swings.

## How many people will live through this hotter century?

India’s population has grown from 43.6 crore in 1960 to 145.1 crore in 2024. More people means more bodies to shield from heat, more mouths to feed from an uncertain harvest, and more homes in the path of storms and floods. The scale of exposure, measured in plain human numbers, is what makes every fraction of a degree of warming matter for India.

---

The data shows a country already living with a changed climate: hotter, more humid, with wilder rain and rising seas. It also shows a country that is still a small part of the cause, per person and historically, but whose emissions are growing fast. The hardest truth is the number of people, 145 crore and counting, who face these shifts with livelihoods tied to the land. The numbers do not predict the future; they show what is already happening and what will likely get worse without a sharp turn in the world’s energy use.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: A comprehensive walk through India’s changing climate: physical signals like temperature, humidity, rainfall, and sea level; the local extremes of heat and poor air; the country’s emissions, both total and per person, alongside historical responsibility; and the enormous human exposure from a large farming workforce and a population of 1.45 billion.

pullQuotes.quote: India’s per-person CO₂ is 2.2 tonnes, less than a sixth of the American figure.
The US has put 435 billion tonnes of CO₂ into the air; India, 66 billion.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Imagine you weigh yourself every morning and, instead of saying '70 kg', you say '+1 kg' or '-2 kg' compared to your usual weight. A temperature anomaly is exactly that, the difference between the temperature on a given day (or month or year) and a long-term average, known as the baseline. For this page, the baseline is 1991–2020. So an anomaly of +0.5°C means the temperature was half a degree warmer than the average for that period. It does not mean the temperature was 0.5°C. It means it was 0.5°C above normal.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Almost every warming chart on this page uses anomalies because they cancel out the huge natural differences between seasons and regions, revealing the human-caused signal clearly.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Picture a joint family of ten that uses five LPG cylinders a month. The total cylinders (5) is a big number, but the per-person number (0.5) tells you how much each member actually consumes. Per capita emissions divide a country’s total CO₂ by its population. It is the fairness measure because it accounts for the fact that a country with more people will naturally emit more total CO₂, but each person may still emit very little.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India’s per capita emissions of 2.2 tonnes are far below those of rich countries, so the total emissions picture alone can mislead.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Think of a bathtub. The current level is not just from the water added this minute; it is the sum of all water added over time. CO₂ stays in the atmosphere for centuries, so the warming we feel today is caused by the total stock of CO₂ put up there since the Industrial Revolution. Cumulative emissions add up every year’s emissions since 1750. A country that started burning coal in 1850 has a much larger historical contribution than one that started later.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India’s cumulative total is only 66 billion tonnes, while the US has 435 billion. The atmosphere cares about the stock, and the historical responsibility chart makes that clear.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: AQI is a colour-coded number that tells you how clean or polluted the air is right now. It takes the concentrations of several pollutants (PM2.5, PM10, ozone, etc.) and converts the worst one into a single scale. A value of 0–50 is good, 51–100 moderate, 101–150 unhealthy for sensitive groups, and above 150 unhealthy for everyone. A number like 156 in Mumbai means the air is risky to breathe for long periods.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The live AQI snapshot for five Indian cities shows that the same fossil-fuel burning that warms the planet also dirties the air people breathe every day.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: PM2.5 stands for particulate matter that is smaller than 2.5 micrometres, about 30 times thinner than a human hair. These tiny particles, often from vehicle exhaust, industrial smoke, and dust, can travel deep into the lungs and even enter the bloodstream. Unlike AQI, which is a live reading, the mean annual exposure is an average over a full year, showing the long-term pollution load on a population.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The chart on long-term PM2.5 exposure shows that India’s air, while slightly improving, is still far above safe levels, affecting hundreds of millions of people.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Think of two different stoves cooking the same meal. One uses a lot of firewood and produces a cloud of smoke; the other uses induction and has no local smoke. Carbon intensity measures how much CO₂ is released to produce one unit of electricity (one kilowatt-hour). A high number means the grid is dirty; a low number means it is cleaner. It is the single best measure of whether the power sector is getting greener, regardless of how much electricity is used.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India’s carbon intensity has fallen from 740 to 670 gCO₂/kWh, showing some progress, but it is still high because coal dominates.

visualizationDecisions.reason: A line chart with yearly markers best shows the long-term trend and recent acceleration on mobile.
AQI is a live reading; a compact card with color and number is instantly readable.
Comparing a few countries is clearest with horizontal bars on mobile.

chartExplainers.title: How much hotter India is than normal
chartExplainers.takeaway: India’s annual temperature anomaly has swung from –0.92°C in 1940 to +0.57°C in 2024, a warming of about 1.5°C.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart plots the temperature anomaly each year against the 1991–2020 baseline. In the 1940s and 1950s, the bars are mostly blue, meaning cooler than normal. By the 1980s, they begin to flip to red, and since the 1990s, nearly every year has been above the baseline. The highest anomaly in this record is 0.66°C in 2016. Because the baseline itself is already warmer than the earlier decades, even a small positive anomaly signals significant extra heat. The national average hides much larger spikes in specific regions and cities, especially during summer.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: This is the most direct answer to 'Is India getting hotter?' and anchors the whole page.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look for the transition from blue to red bars and the upward trend line; the value is the gap from the 1991–2020 average.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read a small recent anomaly as 'little warming'; the baseline period was already warm.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a simple line chart with only the zero line and major year labels.

chartExplainers.title: A century of warming, one stripe per year
chartExplainers.takeaway: The climate stripes show an unmistakable shift from blue to deep red, with the last two decades almost entirely red.
chartExplainers.detail: Each vertical stripe represents one year’s average temperature anomaly, coloured from dark blue (cool) through white (normal) to dark red (warm). The first half of the record is a mix of blues and whites. The reds appear occasionally in the 1980s and then dominate from the 1990s onward. The deepest reds correspond to the warmest years, like 2016 and 2024. No numbers are shown, just the colour pattern, making the trend instantly visible to anyone.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Stripes are the most intuitive way to communicate warming without needing to understand axes or units.
chartExplainers.howToRead: See the left-to-right colour shift from blue to red; the deeper and more frequent the red, the warmer it was.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not think a single blue stripe in recent years means cooling; natural variability still exists.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Stripes work well as a horizontal strip; ensure the colour contrast is strong in dark mode.

chartExplainers.title: Each decade hotter than the last
chartExplainers.takeaway: Every decade since the 1970s has been warmer than the previous one, with the 2010s averaging 0.3°C above the baseline.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart groups the anomalies into ten-year averages. The first three decades (1940s to 1960s) all sit below the baseline, with the 1940s around –0.7°C. The 1970s rise to near zero, and from the 1990s onward, each decade is firmly above zero. The 2010s reach +0.3°C, and the partial 2020s already exceed that. The step-like rise shows that the warming is not cyclical; it is a staircase.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Decade averages smooth out the noise of individual years, making the long-term trend irrefutable.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each bar is a decade; the trend is the bar heights rising monotonically.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not compare a single year to a decade; the point is the persistent decadal climb.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars with decade labels are compact and phone-friendly.

chartExplainers.title: India's actual average temperature, not just the gap
chartExplainers.takeaway: The land-only average temperature has risen from 22.96°C in 1940 to 24.46°C in 2024.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart plots the absolute average temperature from the ERA5 reanalysis, which estimates the temperature over land. The raw number is measured in degrees Celsius, not as an anomaly. The line climbs steadily, with a dip in the 1970s and then a sharp rise after 2000. While 24°C sounds mild, remember this is an all-India, all-year mean, it combines the Himalayan winter with the peak summer months. The real meaning is in the change: a 1.5°C rise in such a short time.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It gives the absolute context for the anomaly charts and shows that India already lives in a hot climate.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The y-axis is in degrees Celsius; the trend is the upward direction.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not compare this number to a daily temperature in your city; it is a heavily averaged value.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A simple line with a shaded trend band works well.

chartExplainers.title: Where India's heat is headed, to 2100
chartExplainers.takeaway: Under a middle-emissions scenario, India’s average temperature could reach 26.64°C by 2100, a further 2°C rise.
chartExplainers.detail: This multi-line chart shows observed temperatures from 1950 to 2014, then projections under three scenarios. The low-emissions path (SSP1-2.6) keeps the rise to about 1°C from 2015 levels. The high-emissions path (SSP5-8.5) pushes it to 29.25°C, almost 4.6°C above today. The middle path (SSP2-4.5) is the most likely current trajectory. All scenarios show clear warming, but the difference between them is stark: the future is not fixed.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It makes the stakes tangible: the choice of global emissions will determine whether India warms a little more or a lot more.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Follow the historical line then pick a future line; the y-axis is degrees Celsius.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not treat the highest scenario as a prediction; it is a 'what if' based on very high emissions.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Limit lines to three or four and use distinct, colour-blind friendly colours.

chartExplainers.title: It is not just the heat, it is the humidity
chartExplainers.takeaway: India’s average relative humidity rose from 51.5% in 1940 to 64.2% in 2024.
chartExplainers.detail: Relative humidity measures how much moisture the air holds compared to its maximum at that temperature. A rise of 12 percentage points is significant for a country-wide annual average. This upward trend means that even a day with the same temperature will feel heavier and more oppressive. In coastal cities like Mumbai and Chennai, humidity is the key to heat stress: a 35°C day with 80% humidity can be more dangerous than a 45°C dry day because the body cannot cool itself.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Heat and humidity together is the real health risk, especially for outdoor workers.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look at the long-term upward trend; the y-axis is relative humidity in percent.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read this as daily humidity; it is an annual average that smooths seasonal swings.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A line chart with a trend line is sufficient.

chartExplainers.title: How India's rainfall is swinging
chartExplainers.takeaway: Annual precipitation has increased from about 1,212 mm in 1940 to 1,404 mm in 2025, but with huge year-to-year swings.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart shows total annual precipitation in millimetres. The long-term trend is slightly upward, but the year-to-year variability is the real story. 2018 saw just 1,110 mm, while 2019 jumped to 1,375 mm. Such swings make farming and water management extremely difficult. The monsoon is not just about the total; the distribution of rain within the season matters just as much, and this chart shows that even the annual total is unsteady.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: A stable-looking average hides violent volatility that farmers depend on.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Note the ups and downs; the trend line is secondary.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not interpret a rising trend as 'more water'; irregular timing can mean floods and droughts in the same year.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A line chart with sparse y-axis labels works well.

chartExplainers.title: What the air feels like across India today
chartExplainers.takeaway: The live AQI ranges from 58 in Bengaluru (moderate) to 156 in Mumbai (unhealthy).
chartExplainers.detail: This comparison chart shows the latest Air Quality Index readings for five Indian cities. Mumbai’s AQI of 156 falls in the 'unhealthy' category; Delhi’s 95 and Chennai’s 73 are 'moderate'; Kolkata’s 65 is 'moderate'; Bengaluru’s 58 is 'moderate'. The dominant pollutant in all these readings is PM2.5. These numbers are snapshots from a single day and will change, but they represent a typical pattern. The same fossil fuel burning that drives climate change also emits these fine particles.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It connects the climate story to the air people breathe right now.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each bar represents one city; the colour and height indicate the severity.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not compare these numbers to annual averages; they are live readings.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use compact horizontal bars with AQI colour codes.

chartExplainers.title: ...and how dirty the air has been, year after year
chartExplainers.takeaway: Annual average PM2.5 exposure fell from 62 µg/m³ in 1990 to 48.4 µg/m³ in 2020, but remains over nine times the WHO guideline.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart shows the mean annual exposure to PM2.5 across India. The slow decline is likely due to cleaner cooking fuels and some industrial regulations. However, 48 µg/m³ is still extremely high, the WHO recommends an annual average of no more than 5 µg/m³. This means that, despite progress, the average Indian is breathing air that poses serious health risks. The improvement, while real, is far from sufficient.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It provides the long-term context missing from a single-day AQI snapshot.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The downward trend is positive, but note the y-axis scale: the values are still very high.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not celebrate the decline as 'air is clean'; it is only less dirty.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A line chart with a shaded 'safe' zone (e.g., 5 µg/m³) would be helpful.

chartExplainers.title: The local heat the national average hides: very hot days
chartExplainers.takeaway: The number of days reaching 35°C or more has fallen in most cities, with Delhi dropping from 144 to 87 days.
chartExplainers.detail: This multi-line chart tracks days per year with maximum temperature ≥35°C for five cities. Delhi, which had 144 such days in 1940, saw 87 in 2025. Chennai dropped from 28 to 21. Mumbai, Kolkata, and Bengaluru have very few. The declines may reflect local aerosol pollution masking warming, changes in wind, or other factors. This threshold alone does not capture heat stress, because humidity and night-time temperatures have risen, as the next chart shows.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It reveals that 'hot days' are not increasing everywhere, complicating the simple warming narrative.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each line is a city; most show a decline.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not conclude that heat risk is decreasing; the danger has shifted from daytime heat to humid heat and warm nights.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Limit to the three most distinct cities to avoid clutter.

chartExplainers.title: And the nights are not cooling down
chartExplainers.takeaway: Chennai’s warm nights surged from 22 in 1940 to 99 in 2025; Mumbai’s rose from 2 to 14.
chartExplainers.detail: This multi-line chart shows how many nights per year stay above a comfort threshold. Chennai shows a dramatic rise of 77 additional uncomfortably warm nights. Mumbai’s number has also risen sharply. Delhi’s count is more variable but trendless. Warm nights prevent the body from recovering from daytime heat, making heatwaves far more deadly. The rise in coastal cities, where humidity is high, is particularly worrying.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Hot nights are a known killer; this chart exposes a clear and dangerous trend in several Indian cities.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look for the upward trend, especially in Chennai; note that Delhi’s line is flatter.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not interpret Delhi’s flat trend as good news; local data can be noisy, and heat stress is rising elsewhere.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Group the cities; Chennai and Mumbai in one chart, Delhi optionally.

chartExplainers.title: The sea is rising on India's coasts
chartExplainers.takeaway: Mumbai sea level rose 232 mm from 1878 to 2024; Chennai rose 315 mm from 1916 to 2022.
chartExplainers.detail: These two line charts show annual mean sea level from tide gauges. Mumbai’s record runs from 1878, Chennai’s from 1916. Both show a clear upward trend. The rise is not constant; there are small year-to-year variations, but the long-term direction is unmistakable. A sea that was 192 mm higher in 2024 means storm surges reach further inland, and low-lying areas like Mumbai’s reclaimed land and Chennai’s coast are more frequently flooded.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Direct, instrument-measured sea-level rise is one of the most tangible impacts of a warming planet.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each chart is a time series; the y-axis is mm relative to 1961-1990.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume the rate is constant; it appears to be accelerating in recent decades.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Two small line charts stacked vertically are better than one complex one.

chartExplainers.title: The human toll of climate-linked disasters
chartExplainers.takeaway: In 2025, floods, landslides, and storms caused 717 deaths; extreme temperatures killed 733 in 2024.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart stacks deaths by disaster type annually. Floods are the most consistent killer, with landslides and storms also significant. Extreme temperature deaths are more variable; 2024 was particularly bad. The numbers do not show a clear trend, highlighting that disaster mortality depends on many factors, including warning systems and shelter. But the underlying climate risks, heavier rain, hotter extremes, rising seas, are loading the dice.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It puts a human face on the physical changes and shows that these are not just abstract numbers.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The bars are stacked by disaster type; the total height is the total deaths.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read a single year as a trend; disaster deaths are highly variable.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A stacked bar chart showing only recent years is clearest on mobile.

chartExplainers.title: How much CO2 India now emits each year
chartExplainers.takeaway: Annual CO₂ emissions reached 3.19 billion tonnes in 2024, up from 3.9 lakh tonnes in 1858.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart shows the exponential rise in carbon dioxide emissions from fossil fuels and industry. The curve bends upward sharply after 1970 and steepens after 2000. The increase in just the last decade, from 2.23 billion tonnes in 2015 to 3.19 billion in 2024, matches the rise of coal-fired power and industry. This is the production-based measure, meaning it counts what is emitted on Indian territory.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It is the starting point for any discussion of India’s role in climate change.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The y-axis is in tonnes; note the log-like shape of the line.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse annual emissions with cumulative or per capita emissions.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A line chart with sparse y-axis labels (e.g., 1B, 2B, 3B) is fine.

chartExplainers.title: CO2 is not the whole story: the gas mix
chartExplainers.takeaway: India’s total greenhouse gases in 2024 were 4.26 billion tonnes CO₂-equivalent, with methane making up 20%.
chartExplainers.detail: This column chart breaks down emissions into CO₂ (3.09 billion tonnes), methane (859 million tonnes CO₂e), and nitrous oxide (310 million tonnes CO₂e). Methane is a much more potent but shorter-lived greenhouse gas than CO₂, and it comes mainly from agriculture (rice paddies and livestock) and waste. Nitrous oxide also arises from fertilizer use. This mix is important because reducing non-CO₂ gases can yield fast reductions in warming.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It corrects the narrow focus on CO₂ and highlights the significant role of agriculture.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The stacked columns show the three gases; CO₂ dominates but the other two are substantial.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not measure only CO₂ and think you have the full climate impact.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A stacked bar for the latest year with a small line for historical context works.

chartExplainers.title: Where India's emissions actually come from
chartExplainers.takeaway: Electricity and heat are the largest emitting sector at 1.55 billion tonnes CO₂e, followed by agriculture at 809 million.
chartExplainers.detail: This stacked chart allocates total greenhouse gases to sectors. Electricity and heat dominate because of coal. Agriculture is next, reflecting India’s large rural workforce. Manufacturing, transport, and buildings follow. The land-use category is negative, meaning India’s forests and land are net absorbers of CO₂. This sectoral pattern tells a story different from rich countries: industrial emissions are still growing, but farm emissions are already large and hard to reduce.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: To understand where emissions come from, which dictates which solutions are relevant.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The size of each section shows its contribution; the trend over time matters too.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume that reducing electricity emissions alone will solve the problem; agriculture and industry matter hugely.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A composition stack for the latest year with a line chart of the top sectors over time.

chartExplainers.title: Is India's growth pulling away from its emissions?
chartExplainers.takeaway: GDP grew 7.5x and CO₂ emissions 5.5x from 1990 to 2024, indicating relative decoupling.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart indexes both GDP and CO₂ emissions to a common start year. The two lines rise together but diverge slightly. GDP outpaces emissions, meaning the carbon intensity of the economy (CO₂ per rupee of GDP) is falling. This is progress, but it is not absolute decoupling: emissions are still rising, just more slowly than output. The dream is for emissions to peak and decline while GDP grows, that has not happened yet.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It answers the important development-versus-climate question: can India grow richer without cooking the planet?
chartExplainers.howToRead: Two indexed lines starting at 100; the gap between them widening is a good sign.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not misinterpret 'relative decoupling' as 'emissions are falling'; they are still rising.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A simple two-line chart with shading is ideal.

chartExplainers.title: Is India's electricity getting cleaner?
chartExplainers.takeaway: Carbon intensity fell from 740 gCO₂/kWh in 2000 to 670 in 2025, a 9% drop.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart shows the grams of CO₂ emitted per unit of electricity generated each year. The long, slow decline reflects the growing share of renewables and nuclear, along with more efficient coal plants. However, the drop is modest, just 70 grams in 25 years. To meet climate goals, this number needs to fall much faster. The good news is that the recent acceleration in solar and wind capacity suggests the curve may steepen.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The carbon intensity of power is the single most important metric for the energy transition.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Downward is good; the pace of decline is what matters.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume that a falling carbon intensity means total emissions are falling; if electricity demand grows fast, total emissions can still rise.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A line with a clear downward trend and recent values labeled.

chartExplainers.title: How India makes its electricity, and how it is shifting
chartExplainers.takeaway: Coal still generated 70.8% of electricity in 2025, but solar and wind are growing rapidly.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart plots generation by source over time. Coal’s line dwarfs all others, but solar’s line has surged from near zero in 2010 to 97 TWh in 2025. Wind and hydro are also significant. The clean share reached 26.7%. The challenge is that total demand is growing so fast that even large clean additions have only managed to slow the growth of coal, not reduce it in absolute terms.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It visualises the core tension of India’s energy transition: clean energy is booming, but coal still rules.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look at the different coloured lines; solar’s steep rise is the key story.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not look only at percentage shares; absolute generation of coal continues to rise.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Show the top four fuels as lines; stack the rest in an 'other' category.

chartExplainers.title: The clean-energy surge: capacity from near zero
chartExplainers.takeaway: Solar capacity reached 97.4 GW in 2024, up from nearly zero in 2000; total renewables hit 204 GW.
chartExplainers.detail: This multi-line chart shows installed capacity for solar, wind, and all renewables. Solar’s line is almost vertical after 2015. Wind shows steady growth. These numbers represent the maximum possible generation if all plants ran at full tilt. Actual generation is lower because the sun doesn’t always shine, but capacity is the hardware. India’s solar build-out is one of the fastest in the world.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It shows the foundation being laid for a cleaner grid, even if current generation remains coal-heavy.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The lines rise steeply, especially after 2015; the y-axis is in gigawatts.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse installed capacity with actual electricity generation; they are different.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Three lines with clear labels; a log scale might help show the earlier years.

chartExplainers.title: But coal still rules the grid
chartExplainers.takeaway: Coal’s share of total generation was 70.8% in 2025, while the clean share was 26.7%.
chartExplainers.detail: This share chart shows that despite the surge in renewables, coal still provides over two-thirds of India’s electricity. The clean share has grown from a few percent two decades ago, but it has not yet eaten into coal’s absolute dominance. The reason is simple: total electricity demand doubled in the last decade, and coal met most of that growth.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It provides the honest counterpoint to the clean energy growth story.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The pie or stacked bar is mostly one colour: that colour is coal.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read the clean share as a failure; it’s growing fast but from a small base.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A doughnut or stacked bar with big labels works.

chartExplainers.title: ...but how much per person, next to others?
chartExplainers.takeaway: India’s 2.2 tonnes per person is well below China’s 8.7, the US’s 14.2, and the world average of 4.7.
chartExplainers.detail: This bar chart compares per capita CO₂ emissions across countries in 2024. India’s bar is the shortest among the large economies shown. The US bar is over six times longer. This visual makes the case for fairness: while India’s total emissions are large because of its population, the average Indian is a tiny emitter. The chart does not show other low-income countries, which are even lower.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It is the essential fairness metric in the climate debate.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare the bar lengths; India’s is clearly the smallest among the big four.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not use this to excuse India’s total emissions; the problem is global, but responsibility differs.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars with large country flags or names.

chartExplainers.title: Total emissions: big, but in proportion
chartExplainers.takeaway: India’s 3.19 billion tonnes ranks third globally, behind China and the US.
chartExplainers.detail: This bar chart shows absolute CO₂ emissions in 2024. China’s bar is the tallest; India’s is less than a third of that. The US and EU follow. India’s total emissions are large, but the country’s population is also large. When you see India third, remember that per person it is much lower.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It provides the global ranking without overstating India’s contribution relative to its population.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare the absolute sizes; India is significant but not the biggest.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read 'third largest' as the whole story; per capita and historical are equally important.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars, sorted by size.

chartExplainers.title: ...and who actually filled the atmosphere?
chartExplainers.takeaway: The US has emitted 435 billion tonnes of CO₂ cumulatively; India, 66 billion.
chartExplainers.detail: This bar chart shows cumulative CO₂ since 1750. The US bar is the longest, followed by the EU and China. India’s cumulative total is one-sixth that of the US. This measure is the honest accounting of historical responsibility: the CO₂ that is warming the planet now was emitted over the past 150 years, mostly by today’s rich countries.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It shifts the conversation from current flows to total stock, which is what the atmosphere sees.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The total length of each bar represents centuries of emissions; India’s is relatively short.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not forget that this is cumulative, not annual.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars with country names.

chartExplainers.title: Per-person emissions, four very different paths
chartExplainers.takeaway: India’s per capita emissions have only recently crossed 2 tonnes, while the US has stayed above 10 tonnes since 1900.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart traces per capita emissions from 1750 to today for India, China, the US, and the world. India’s line is a flat line near zero until the 1970s, then a slow incline. China’s rises sharply after 2000. The US line has been high for over a century. This long view reveals that today’s gap is not new; it is rooted in different industrial paths.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It puts the current fairness numbers in a century-spanning context.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each country is a differently coloured line; the y-axis is tonnes per person.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not compare a single year in isolation; the shape of the curves matters.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A line chart with four lines; label the end points.

chartExplainers.title: India's slice of the world's emissions
chartExplainers.takeaway: India’s share of global CO₂ rose from 0.1% in 1858 to 8.3% in 2024.
chartExplainers.detail: This single-series line chart shows India’s share of world total CO₂. It was negligible until the 1980s, then began a slow climb. The acceleration after 2000 reflects India’s rapid economic growth and coal expansion. 8.3% is significant, but India’s population share is about 18%, so the emissions share is still lower than the population share.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It frames India’s role in the collective problem and its leverage in the solution.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line rises; the important number is where it ends.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not think 'only 8%', that is a large slice of a shrinking global budget.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A simple line chart with the latest percentage in a callout.

chartExplainers.title: Why India is so exposed: who works the land
chartExplainers.takeaway: 41.6% of India’s workforce was still in agriculture in 2025, down from 63.1% in 1991.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart shows the share of total employment in agriculture. The long-term decline is steady but slow. In 2025, over two in five workers still depend on farming, forestry, or fishing. These jobs are directly exposed to heat, rainfall variability, and soil moisture changes. A bad monsoon year does not just mean higher food prices; it means millions of households lose income.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The percentage farming is the single most important number for understanding India’s climate vulnerability.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Downward is good, but note the current value is still high.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not think a lower share means fewer farmers; absolute numbers may still be high.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A line chart with a clear downward trend.

chartExplainers.title: ...and how many people face the hotter century
chartExplainers.takeaway: India’s population reached 145.1 crore in 2024, up from 43.6 crore in 1960.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart shows the rapid population growth over six decades. The steepest part of the curve was between 1970 and 2010. The number of people now, nearly 1.5 billion, means that the exposure to climate impacts is enormous. More people in vulnerable coastal cities, more demand for water and food, and more bodies physiologically stressed by heat.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It quantifies the human scale of the climate challenge.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line rises; the number at the end is the key figure.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume this is a projection; it is historical through 2024.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A line with big, rounded numbers on the y-axis.

sourceNotes: Temperature, precipitation, and emissions data from Our World in Data (OWID), based on sources like the Global Carbon Budget and ERA5.
AQI snapshots from the World Air Quality Index project (WAQI).
PM2.5 exposure from the World Bank.
Climate projections from the Climate Change Knowledge Portal (CCKP) of the World Bank, using CMIP6 models.
Electricity data from Ember and the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA).
Sea level data from the Permanent Service for Mean Sea Level (PSMSL).
Disaster deaths from the Centre for Research on the Epidemiology of Disasters (CRED) via OWID.
City-level heat days and nights from Open-Meteo using ERA5 reanalysis.
caveats: Temperature anomalies are relative to the 1991–2020 baseline, not pre-industrial levels.
The national annual average temperature smooths out extreme local heat events.
ERA5 reanalysis used for city-level data may differ from ground station records.
AQI readings are instantaneous snapshots and do not represent long-term averages.
Projections are scenarios, not predictions, and depend on uncertain future emissions.
Disaster death data is based on reported events and may be incomplete.
Per capita emissions are averages and mask differences within the population.
Kolkata very hot days and hot nights have very limited data points in this evidence set.
lockedNumbersUsed: Annual temperature anomaly: Temperature anomaly (1940 and 2024)
Sea level at Mumbai (since 1878) (latest and earliest)
Sea level at Chennai (since 1916) (latest and earliest)
Deaths from natural disasters by type: All disasters (2025, 2024)
qualityFlags: Kolkata very hot days and hot nights have only one observation (2025) in this evidence, making trend analysis impossible for that city.
Some city-level heat indices show declines that may be due to aerosol masking or data inhomogeneities rather than actual cooling.
Projections are from a single model ensemble; other models may give slightly different ranges.
The air quality chapter would benefit from more comprehensive historical PM2.5 data beyond 2020.

plannedCharts.title: How much hotter India is than normal
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The single clearest sign that India's climate is shifting, not just its weather.

plannedCharts.title: A century of warming, one stripe per year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The warming trend with no axes to hide behind, the famous climate-stripes view.

plannedCharts.title: Each decade hotter than the last
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Smoothing year-to-year noise into decade averages shows the staircase of warming.

plannedCharts.title: India's actual average temperature, not just the gap
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Anomalies show change; this shows the real temperature India lives in, land-only.

plannedCharts.title: Where India's heat is headed, to 2100
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank CCKP · CMIP6 · observed and projected average temperature
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Today's warming is small next to where the century is heading, and how hot it gets is still a choice.

plannedCharts.title: It is not just the heat, it is the humidity
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Humid heat is what makes warming dangerous for the body; dry heat and wet heat are not the same threat.

plannedCharts.title: How India's rainfall is swinging
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Warming does not just raise heat, it makes rain less predictable, and India runs on the monsoon.

plannedCharts.title: What the air feels like across India today
plannedCharts.subtitle: WAQI · live air quality index · latest reading
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The same fossil-fuel burning that warms the planet also fills the air people breathe right now.

plannedCharts.title: ...and how dirty the air has been, year after year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Today's snapshot means more next to the long run, and India's average exposure sits far above what is considered safe.

plannedCharts.title: The local heat the national average hides: very hot days
plannedCharts.subtitle: Open-Meteo ERA5 · days per year at or above 35°C
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A one-degree national rise lands as many more dangerously hot days in specific cities, which is what people actually feel.

plannedCharts.title: And the nights are not cooling down
plannedCharts.subtitle: Open-Meteo ERA5 · warm nights per year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Hot nights are a hidden killer: without a cool night the body never recovers from daytime heat.

plannedCharts.title: The sea is rising on India's coasts
plannedCharts.subtitle: PSMSL tide gauges · annual mean sea level
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India has a 7,500 km coastline and major coastal cities, so sea-level rise is a direct national exposure.

plannedCharts.title: The human toll of climate-linked disasters
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Warming loads the dice on the floods, cyclones, and heatwaves that kill people.

plannedCharts.title: How much CO2 India now emits each year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: To read India's climate fairly you have to see its part in the cause, not just the effect.

plannedCharts.title: CO2 is not the whole story: the gas mix
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's farms and livestock make methane and nitrous oxide unusually large, so CO2 alone undercounts.

plannedCharts.title: Where India's emissions actually come from
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The path to lower emissions depends entirely on which sectors dominate.

plannedCharts.title: Is India's growth pulling away from its emissions?
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Whether emissions can fall while incomes rise is the whole development-and-climate question.

plannedCharts.title: Is India's electricity getting cleaner?
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Whether the cause is easing depends on how dirty each unit of power is, not just how much is used.

plannedCharts.title: How India makes its electricity, and how it is shifting
plannedCharts.subtitle: Ember · annual electricity generation by source
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The whole climate question for India is whether clean power can grow faster than rising demand.

plannedCharts.title: The clean-energy surge: capacity from near zero
plannedCharts.subtitle: EIA · installed electricity capacity
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's solar build-out is one of the fastest energy stories anywhere, and it is recent.

plannedCharts.title: But coal still rules the grid
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The surge is real, but the honest headline is that fossil power still does most of the work.

plannedCharts.title: ...but how much per person, next to others?
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Per-person emissions are the fairness number, and they only mean something next to China, the US, and the world.

plannedCharts.title: Total emissions: big, but in proportion
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The headline 'third largest emitter' needs the company it keeps to be read honestly.

plannedCharts.title: ...and who actually filled the atmosphere?
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Today's warming comes from more than a century of emissions, so historical responsibility is the honest accounting.

plannedCharts.title: Per-person emissions, four very different paths
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The gap is not new; the trajectories show how differently these economies grew.

plannedCharts.title: India's slice of the world's emissions
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Climate is a shared problem, so India's share of the global total frames its responsibility and its leverage.

plannedCharts.title: Why India is so exposed: who works the land
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Heat and erratic rain hit hardest where livelihoods depend on the sky, and a huge share of Indians still farm.

plannedCharts.title: ...and how many people face the hotter century
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The stakes of every degree are measured in people, and India has more of them than almost anywhere.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
